sources.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Captures the whole aims and learning outcomes statement as a single cross-cutting official requirement.
Detailed numbered content points are not present in this chunk; they appear in section 2f.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.7, 2a Content of A Level in Mathematics B (H640)

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification
evidence.locator: p.7, 2a Content of A Level in Mathematics B (H640)

reviewNotes: High-level summary of the Pure mathematics content area: proof, algebra, graphs, sequences, trigonometry, logarithms, calculus and vectors.
Pure content is assessed in all three components.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.7, 2a Content of A Level in Mathematics B (H640)

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification
evidence.locator: p.7, 2a Content of A Level in Mathematics B (H640)

reviewNotes: High-level summary of the Mechanics content area: kinematics, motion under gravity, forces including friction, Newton's laws and simple moments.
Mechanics is explicitly assessed only in component 01.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: High-level summary of the Statistics content area: sampling and inference, probability, binomial and Normal distribution models, and hypothesis testing.
Statistics is explicitly assessed only in component 02.
Cross-cutting expectation of technology use across pure, statistics and mechanics; not tied to a specific paper.
Use graphing software to investigate relationships between graphical and algebraic representations.
Use spreadsheets for tables of values, numerical methods, statistics and mechanics modelling.
Use statistical software to explore data sets, generate tables and diagrams, and perform standard calculations.
Use graphing and/or spreadsheet software for mechanics modelling including kinematics and projectiles.
Optional use of CAS to investigate algebraic relationships including derivatives and integrals.
Calculator requirements and examination advice; assessment administration rather than content.
Command word defining expectation to give exact answers with surds, e or pi.
Formal proof requiring high level of mathematical detail and concise conclusion.
Given result must be shown true with sufficiently detailed working covering every step.
Clear substitution of given value to justify a statement.
Command word indicating no justification required; calculator use permitted.
Command word requiring justification and appropriate working.
Command words indicating neither working nor justification is required.
Instruction requiring detailed and complete analytical method leading to a conclusion.
Indicates the next step should be based on what has gone before.
Indicates valid alternate methods exist but the indicated starting point is suggested.
Permissive phrase indicating a given result that learners may use.
Mark points accurately and join with curve, line or line of best fit.
Draw diagram showing main features of a curve including turning points, asymptotes and intercepts.
Draw to accuracy appropriate to the problem, e.g. force diagrams or lines of best fit.
Construct and present mathematical arguments using diagrams, graphs, deduction and precise symbolic language.
Understand and use mathematical language and syntax as set out in the content.
Understand and use set theory language and symbols, applied to inequalities and probability.
Understand and use the definition of a function and domain/range.
Comprehend and critique mathematical arguments, proofs and justifications of methods and formulae.
Recognise underlying mathematical structure and simplify/abstract appropriately.
Construct extended arguments to solve unstructured problems including problems in context.
Interpret and communicate solutions in the context of the original problem.
Understand that numerical methods permit solution to required accuracy when analytical methods fail.
Evaluate accuracy or limitations of solutions including numerical-method solutions.
Understand the mathematical problem-solving cycle: specify, collect, process/represent, interpret.
Understand, interpret and extract information from diagrams and construct mathematical diagrams including in mechanics.
Translate a situation in context into a mathematical model, making simplifying assumptions.
Use a mathematical model with suitable inputs to engage with and explore situations.
Interpret the outputs of a mathematical model in the context of the original situation.
Understand that models can be refined by considering outputs and assumptions; evaluate appropriateness.
Understand and use modelling assumptions.
Proof by deduction and exhaustion; logical structure

Opportunity, systematic, stratified, quota, cluster and self-selected sampling; no dedicated node.
Selecting and evaluating sampling techniques; identifying bias and practicalities.
Covers categorical/discrete/continuous/ranked data and standard single-variable diagrams; some diagram types (dot plot, frequency chart) are not represented as separate nodes.
Histogram area proportional to frequency and estimation of probabilities from area.
Interpretation of cumulative frequency diagrams.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for describing distribution shape, symmetry, unimodal/bimodal or skewness.
reviewNotes: Outcome is about vocabulary of frequency-distribution shape; no matching leaf node exists.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for the idea that sample diagrams become more representative of theoretical distributions as sample size increases.
reviewNotes: Concerns sampling variability and convergence of empirical distributions; not mapped to a specific node.
Scatter diagrams, regression/best-fit lines, interpolation and extrapolation; excludes calculation of regression-line equation.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for identifying distinct sub-populations or outliers in scatter diagrams.
reviewNotes: Outlier judgement by eye in scatter diagrams; no specific node.
Describing correlation and understanding that correlation does not imply causation.
Selecting and critiquing presentation techniques including time-series graphs.
Central tendency, interpretation, choice of measure and weighted mean; weighted mean is not a separate node.
Simple spread measures: range, percentiles, quartiles and interquartile range.
Variance and standard deviation for raw, frequency and grouped data; calculator use.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for identifying outliers using the 2-sd or 1.5*IQR rules.
reviewNotes: Outlier rules based on standard deviation and IQR; no outlier node in catalog.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for data cleaning, missing data or errors.
reviewNotes: Cleaning data including missing values, errors and outliers; no matching node.
Calculating probability of an event using equally likely outcomes.
Understanding complementary events and P(not-A).

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for expected frequency calculated from probability.
reviewNotes: Expected frequency = nP(A); not mapped to a specific node.
Use of tree diagrams, sample-space diagrams and Venn diagrams for probabilities.

syllabusPoints.sourceReference: p.53 Mu1 (mutually exclusive and independent events)
reviewNotes: Understanding and using mutually exclusive and independent events.
Adding probabilities for mutually exclusive events.
Multiplying probabilities for independent events, including use of complements.
Mutually exclusive and independent events with associated notation and union formula.
Venn diagrams for up to three events and probabilities for non-mutually-exclusive events.
Conditional probability from formula, tree diagrams, two-way tables, Venn diagrams and sample-space diagrams; excludes reverse conditional probability.
Independence criterion P(B|A)=P(B) and product rule for independent events.
Recognising situations that give rise to a binomial distribution.
Identifying success probability p and notation B(n,p).
Calculating binomial probabilities, including calculator functions.
Understanding and using mean=np; note mentions derivation.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for expected frequencies associated with a binomial distribution.
reviewNotes: Expected frequencies from binomial distribution; not mapped to a distinct node.
Using probability functions and understanding discrete random variable; restricted to simple finite distributions.
Numerical probabilities for simple finite distributions and discrete uniform distribution.
Normal model, continuity correction and recognising when Normal approximation to binomial is appropriate.
Shape of Normal curve, histograms tending to Normal and area as probability.
Linear transformations of Normal variables and standardisation.
Line of symmetry at mean and points of inflection one standard deviation from mean.
Calculating and using Normal probabilities, including calculator functions.
Modelling with probability distributions and critiquing assumptions, including appropriateness of binomial and Normal models.
Language and process of hypothesis testing including null/alternative hypotheses, significance level, test statistic, tail, critical value/region, acceptance region and p-value.
When to apply one-tailed and two-tailed tests.
Sample as basis for population inference and significance level as probability of incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis.
Identifying null and alternative hypotheses for a binomial probability test.
Conducting a binomial hypothesis test and interpreting conclusions in context.
Identifying critical and acceptance regions for a binomial test.
Distribution of sample mean for samples from a Normal population.
Hypothesis test for a single mean using Normal distribution, known variance or large sample.
Identifying critical and acceptance regions for a Normal mean test.
Correlation as measure of linear fit and rank correlation coefficient; learners are not required to know names of particular coefficients.
Using a given correlation coefficient to make an inference about population correlation/association using p-value or critical value.
Language of simplifying assumptions in mechanics (light, smooth, uniform, particle, inextensible, thin, rigid, long term).
Understanding and using the particle model.

syllabusPoints.unmappedReason: No canonical node for SI fundamental quantities and units length, time and mass.
reviewNotes: Fundamental SI units metre, second and kilogram; no specific node.
Derived quantities and units for velocity, acceleration, force and weight; no dedicated units node.
Derived quantity and unit for moment: newton metre.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Defines kinematic terminology for 1-D motion. Mapped to the relevant 1-D kinematics concept nodes; the point spans multiple nodes.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Distinction between position, displacement, distance and distance travelled.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Distinctions between velocity/speed and acceleration/magnitude of acceleration.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Drawing and interpreting kinematics graphs including gradient and area interpretations.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Differentiating position and velocity with respect to time to obtain velocity and acceleration.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Integrating acceleration and velocity with respect to time to obtain velocity and displacement/position.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Recognition of when constant acceleration formulae are appropriate; learners should derive them.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Problem solving combines constant acceleration formulae and calculus; spans several kinematics nodes.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: 2-D kinematics language; no dedicated 2-D kinematics node exists, so mapped to vector position and 1-D kinematic concepts.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Extends 1-D techniques to 2-D using vectors, including calculus and constant acceleration formulae in vector form.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Finding Cartesian equation from time-parametrised position vector components.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Using vectors to solve kinematics problems including relative position and interpreting velocity/acceleration directions.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Modelling projectile motion under gravity using vectors and standard assumptions.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Finding position, velocity, range and maximum height of a projectile.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Finding initial velocity from given information; narrower than the general horizontal/vertical motion node.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Eliminating time to obtain the Cartesian trajectory equation.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: General problem solving with projectiles; broader than individual projectile nodes.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Terminology for types of force and dependence of normal reaction on other forces.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Variation of g with location and modelling g as constant on Earth; no dedicated gravitational-acceleration node exists.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Identifying forces and representing them in force diagrams, including external/internal forces.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Finding resultants of concurrent forces in parallel/perpendicular directions or component-vector form.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Equilibrium as vector sum of forces being zero in specified cases.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Resolving forces into components and finding resultants by adding components.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Equilibrium condition for a particle or body under concurrent forces.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Vectors in equilibrium sum to zero and closed-force-figure representation.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Solving equilibrium problems by resolving or using polygons/triangles of forces.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Contact force as combination of normal contact force and friction; smooth/rough modelling assumptions.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Friction model F_R <= mu N and F_R = mu N when sliding; coefficient of friction and limiting/static friction.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Applying Newton's laws in problems involving friction.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Know and understand all three Newton's laws and apply them; no dedicated first-law node exists.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Understanding the term equation of motion; associated with Newton's second law.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Formulating equations of motion for a particle in a straight line, including motion under gravity and simple 2-D component cases.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Modelling systems as connected particles such as pulley systems and trains.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Formulating equations of motion for individual particles within a connected system.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: System with no relative motion modelled as a single particle of total mass.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Formulating equations of motion for a particle moving in a straight line or in a plane, extending n3.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Calculating moment of a force about a point or axis for rods and rectangular laminas.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Rigid body equilibrium requires zero resultant force and zero sum of moments about any point.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Understanding that forces can exert a turning effect on a rigid body.

evidence.title: OCR A Level Mathematics B MEI H640 specification

reviewNotes: Weight of a body acts through its centre of mass for moment calculations.
Appendix requirement that learners understand standard set notation; paper allocation not explicitly fixed.
Appendix requirement covering common mathematical symbols and sequence notation.
Appendix requirement covering arithmetic, summation, factorial and binomial coefficient notation.
Appendix requirement covering function, limit, derivative and integral notation.
Appendix requirement covering e, exp, log and ln notation.
Appendix requirement covering trigonometric and inverse trigonometric function notation and units.
Appendix requirement covering vector notation in two and three dimensions. Sections 7 and 8 are not present in this chunk.
Appendix requirement covering probability, distribution and statistical notation. Section 10 is not present in this chunk.
Appendix requirement covering SI units and mechanics notation.
